epigraph.text: It is the glory of God to conceal a matter; to search out a matter is the glory of kings.
epigraph.quote: I do not know what I may appear to the world, but to myself I seem to have been only a boy playing on the sea-shore, whilst the great ocean of truth lay all undiscovered before me.

content.text:
In Section [sec:deriv-rates] we computed $f'(a)$ for specific values of 
$a$: the derivative of $x^{2}$ at $a = 1$, at $a = 3$, at $a = -2$. Each 
computation required a fresh limit calculation. But when we collected our 
results—$f'(1) = 2$, $f'(3) = 6$, $f'(-2) = -4$—we noticed that every 
answer had the form $f'(a) = 2a$. In other words, the derivative itself 
is a *function*: feed it an input $x$, and it returns $f'(x) = 2x$.

This is the key insight of the present section. Instead of computing the 
derivative one point at a time, we leave $a$ as a variable and compute $f'(x)$ 
for all $x$ at once. The result is a brand-new function—one that encodes 
the slope of the original curve at every point.

content.title: The Derivative Function
content.content:
The **derivative of $f$** is the 
function $f'$ defined by
\[
 f'(x) = \limtozero{h} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}
\]
at every $x$ for which this limit exists. The domain of $f'$ is the set 
of all $x$ in the domain of $f$ where $f$ is differentiable.

content.text:
The process of computing $f'$ is called **differentiation**. 
We say that we "differentiate $f$" to obtain $f'$.
Computing Derivative Functions from the Definition
We now repeat, more systematically, the computations from Section [sec:deriv-rates]—but this time we keep the variable $x$ throughout.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = x^{2}$
content.solution:
$$
 f'(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{(x+h)^{2} - x^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{x^{2} + 2xh + h^{2} - x^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{2xh + h^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} (2x + h) \\[6pt]
 &= 2x.
$$
The derivative of $f(x) = x^{2}$ is $f'(x) = 2x$. This single formula 
encodes every tangent-slope calculation we did in Section [sec:deriv-rates]: 
$f'(1) = 2$, $f'(3) = 6$, $f'(-2) = -4$, and so on.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = x^{3}$
content.solution:
$$
 f'(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{(x+h)^{3} - x^{3}}{h}.
$$
We expand:
\[
 (x+h)^{3} = x^{3} + 3x^{2}h + 3xh^{2} + h^{3}.
\]
Therefore:
$$
 f'(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{x^{3} + 3x^{2}h + 3xh^{2} + h^{3} - x^{3}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{3x^{2}h + 3xh^{2} + h^{3}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} (3x^{2} + 3xh + h^{2}) \\[6pt]
 &= 3x^{2}.
$$
The derivative of $x^{3}$ is $3x^{2}$.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$
content.solution:
$$
 f'(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{\dfrac{1}{x+h} - \dfrac{1}{x}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{x - (x+h)}{x(x+h)} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{-h}{x(x+h)} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{-1}{x(x+h)} \\[6pt]
 &= \frac{-1}{x \cdot x} \\[6pt]
 &= -\frac{1}{x^{2}}.
$$
The derivative of $\dfrac{1}{x}$ is $-\dfrac{1}{x^{2}}$. Note that $f'$ 
is defined for all $x \neq 0$—the same domain as $f$.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$
content.solution:
$$
 f'(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{\sqrt{x+h} - \sqrt{x}}{h} 
 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{(x+h) - x}{h\bigl(\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}\bigr)} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{h}{h\bigl(\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}\bigr)} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{1}{\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}} \\[6pt]
 &= \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}.
$$
The derivative of $\sqrt{x}$ is $\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$ for $x > 0$. 
Observe that $f'(x) \to \infty$ as $x \to 0^{+}$, which reflects the 
increasingly steep rise of $\sqrt{x}$ near the origin.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = \abs{x}$
content.solution:
We consider three cases.

*Case 1: $x > 0$.** Then for sufficiently small 
$\abs{h}$, $x + h > 0$, so $\abs{x+h} = x + h$ and $\abs{x} = x$:
\[
 f'(x) = \limtozero{h} \frac{(x+h) - x}{h} = \limtozero{h} 1 = 1.
\]

*Case 2: $x < 0$.** Then for sufficiently small 
$\abs{h}$, $x + h < 0$, so $\abs{x+h} = -(x+h)$ and $\abs{x} = -x$:
\[
 f'(x) = \limtozero{h} \frac{-(x+h) - (-x)}{h} 
 = \limtozero{h} \frac{-h}{h} = -1.
\]

*Case 3: $x = 0$.** We showed in 
Section [sec:deriv-rates] that the one-sided limits disagree:
\[
 \limto{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{\abs{h}}{h} = 1, \qquad
 \limto{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{\abs{h}}{h} = -1.
\]
Since $1 \neq -1$, $f'(0)$ does not exist.

Therefore:
\[
 f'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > 0, \\[3pt]
 -1 & \text{if } x < 0, \\[3pt]
 \text{undefined} & \text{if } x = 0.
 \end{cases}
\]
This can be written as $f'(x) = \dfrac{x}{\abs{x}}$ for $x \neq 0$, or 
equivalently $f'(x) = \operatorname{sgn}(x)$.

content.text:
One of the most illuminating exercises is to graph a function and its 
derivative side by side. The derivative $f'(x)$ tells us the slope of $f$ 
at each point, so:

items:
Where $f$ is **increasing** (rising from left to right), the 
 slope is positive, so $f'(x) > 0$.

Where $f$ is **decreasing** (falling from left to right), the 
 slope is negative, so $f'(x) < 0$.

Where $f$ has a **horizontal tangent** (a local peak or valley), 
 the slope is zero, so $f'(x) = 0$.

content.title: Graphing $x^{2}$ and $2x$
content.problem: Sketch $f(x) = x^{2}$ and $f'(x) = 2x$ on the same axes.
content.solution:
center
tikzpicture
 axis[
 axis lines=middle,
 xlabel={$x$},
 ylabel={$y$},
 xmin=-3.5, xmax=3.5,
 ymin=-4, ymax=7,
 width=10cm, height=7cm,
 legend pos=north west,
 legend style={font=},
 samples=100,
 grid=both,
 grid style={AtlasBorder, very thin},
 tick style={AtlasSecondary},
 tick label style={font=, AtlasSecondary},
 ]
 {x^2};
 $f(x) = x^{2}$
 {2*x};
 $f'(x) = 2x$
 axis
tikzpicture
center

Observe:

- For $x < 0$, $f$ is decreasing and $f'(x) = 2x < 0$.

- At $x = 0$, $f$ has its minimum and $f'(0) = 0$.

- For $x > 0$, $f$ is increasing and $f'(x) = 2x > 0$.

- As $x$ moves away from zero, $f$ gets steeper and $\abs{f'(x)}$ 
 grows.
The derivative function *detects* the behavior of the original function.

content.title: Graphing $x^{3}$ and $3x^{2}$
content.solution:
center
tikzpicture
 axis[
 axis lines=middle,
 xlabel={$x$},
 ylabel={$y$},
 xmin=-2.5, xmax=2.5,
 ymin=-5, ymax=10,
 width=10cm, height=7cm,
 legend pos=north west,
 legend style={font=},
 samples=100,
 grid=both,
 grid style={AtlasBorder, very thin},
 tick style={AtlasSecondary},
 tick label style={font=, AtlasSecondary},
 ]
 {x^3};
 $f(x) = x^{3}$
 {3*x^2};
 $f'(x) = 3x^{2}$
 axis
tikzpicture
center

Key observations:

- $f(x) = x^{3}$ is always increasing (for $x \neq 0$), and 
 $f'(x) = 3x^{2} \ge 0$ everywhere.

- At $x = 0$, $f$ has a horizontal tangent and $f'(0) = 0$, even 
 though $f$ is not at a peak or valley. The function merely 
 "levels off" for an instant before continuing to increase.

- $f'(x) = 3x^{2}$ is always nonnegative—it touches zero only at 
 $x = 0$.

content.text:
The derivative is so fundamental that several notations have developed over 
the centuries. Each has its advantages, and you will encounter all of them 
in mathematics and the sciences.

rows: "$dy\,dx$" or "the derivative of $y$ with respect to $x$"

content.text:
The Leibniz notation $\deriv{y}{x}$ is particularly powerful because it 
reminds us what we are differentiating ($y$) and with respect to what ($x$). 
Although $\deriv{y}{x}$ looks like a fraction, it is *not* literally a 
ratio of two numbers $dy$ and $dx$—it is a single symbol denoting the 
limit
\[
 \deriv{y}{x} = \limto{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}
 = \limtozero{h} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}.
\]
The "fraction-like" behavior of Leibniz notation will prove remarkably 
useful when we study the chain rule and integration.

content.title: Leibniz notation is not a fraction
content.content:
Although $\deriv{y}{x}$ behaves like a fraction in many formulas 
(a fact we will exploit later), it is defined as a *limit*. Writing 
$\deriv{y}{x} = \limtozero{h} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ is the precise 
meaning. Do not attempt to "cancel" $dx$ as if it were a standalone 
quantity—at least not yet.

content.text:
When we want to evaluate the derivative at a specific point $x = a$, we 
write
\[
 f'(a), \qquad \deriv{y}{x}\bigg|_{x=a}, \qquad 
 \text{or} \qquad \left.\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[f(x)\bigr]\right|_{x=a}.
\]

Since $f'$ is itself a function, we can differentiate it to obtain the 
**second derivative** of $f$:

content.title: Higher-Order Derivatives
content.content:
The **second derivative** of $f$ is $f'' = (f')'$, the derivative of 
the derivative. In Leibniz notation:
\[
 f''(x) = \dderiv{y}{x} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left[\deriv{y}{x}\right].
\]
More generally, the **$n$th derivative** of $f$ is
\[
 f^{(n)}(x) = \frac{d^{n}y}{dx^{n}},
\]
obtained by differentiating $n$ times.

content.title: Higher derivatives of $x^{3}$
content.solution:
We already know $f'(x) = 3x^{2}$. Now we differentiate $f'$:
$$
 f''(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{3(x+h)^{2} - 3x^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{3(x^{2} + 2xh + h^{2}) - 3x^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{6xh + 3h^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} (6x + 3h) \\[6pt]
 &= 6x.
$$
Continuing:
$$
 f'''(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{6(x+h) - 6x}{h} 
 = \limtozero{h} \frac{6h}{h} = 6, \\[6pt]
 f^{(4)}(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{6 - 6}{h} = 0.
$$
All subsequent derivatives are also zero: $f^{(n)}(x) = 0$ for $n \ge 4$.

content.title: Second derivative of $\sqrt{x}$
content.solution:
We know $f'(x) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}} = \dfrac{1}{2}x^{-1/2}$. Now we 
differentiate $g(x) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$ using the limit definition:
$$
 f''(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{g(x+h) - g(x)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x+h}} - \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{1}{2h} \cdot 
 \frac{\sqrt{x} - \sqrt{x+h}}{\sqrt{x}\,\sqrt{x+h}}.
$$
We rationalize the numerator $\sqrt{x} - \sqrt{x+h}$ by multiplying by 
$\dfrac{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{x+h}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{x+h}}$:
$$
 f''(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{1}{2h} \cdot 
 \frac{x - (x+h)}{\sqrt{x}\,\sqrt{x+h}\,\bigl(\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{x+h}\bigr)} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{1}{2h} \cdot 
 \frac{-h}{\sqrt{x}\,\sqrt{x+h}\,\bigl(\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{x+h}\bigr)} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} 
 \frac{-1}{2\sqrt{x}\,\sqrt{x+h}\,\bigl(\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{x+h}\bigr)} \\[6pt]
 &= \frac{-1}{2\sqrt{x} \cdot \sqrt{x} \cdot 2\sqrt{x}} \\[6pt]
 &= \frac{-1}{4x\sqrt{x}} \\[6pt]
 &= -\frac{1}{4x^{3/2}}.
$$
The second derivative is negative for all $x > 0$, which means the graph of 
$\sqrt{x}$ is always "bending downward"—a property we call 
*concavity*, which we explore in Section 3.3.

content.text:
We have observed that discontinuous functions are never differentiable, and 
that continuous functions (like $\abs{x}$) are not always differentiable. 
The precise relationship is captured by the following theorem.

content.title: Differentiability Implies Continuity
content.content: If $f$ is differentiable at $a$, then $f$ is continuous at $a$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We need to show that $\limto{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$. The key idea is to 
write $f(x) - f(a)$ in a form that involves the derivative. For 
$x \neq a$:
\[
 f(x) - f(a) = \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} \cdot (x - a).
\]
Taking the limit as $x \to a$:
$$
 \limto{x \to a} \bigl[f(x) - f(a)\bigr] 
 &= \limto{x \to a} \left[\frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} \cdot (x - a)\right] \\[6pt]
 &= \left[\limto{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a}\right] 
 \cdot \left[\limto{x \to a} (x - a)\right] \\[6pt]
 &= f'(a) \cdot 0 \\[6pt]
 &= 0.
$$
In the second line we used the product rule for limits; both limits exist 
because $f'(a)$ exists (by hypothesis) and $\limto{x \to a}(x - a) = 0$ 
is immediate. Since $\limto{x \to a}[f(x) - f(a)] = 0$, we conclude that 
$\limto{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$, which is precisely the definition of 
continuity at $a$.

content.title: The converse is false
content.content:
Continuity does *not* imply differentiability. The function 
$f(x) = \abs{x}$ is continuous at $x = 0$ (you can verify that 
$\limto{x \to 0} \abs{x} = 0 = f(0)$), but as we showed in 
Example [ex:deriv-fn-absx], $f'(0)$ does not exist. The corner in the 
graph prevents the existence of a unique tangent line.

In fact, there exist functions that are continuous *everywhere* but 
differentiable *nowhere*—a startling discovery due to Karl 
Weierstrass in 1872. Such functions are infinitely "jagged" at every 
scale, like a coastline that never smooths out no matter how closely you 
zoom in.

content.text:
We can summarize the logical relationship:
\[
 \text{differentiable at } a \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{continuous at } a,
\]
but the reverse implication $\Longleftarrow$ is **false**.

content.caption: The relationship between differentiability and continuity. Differentiability implies continuity (left to middle), but the converse is false: a function can be continuous without being differentiable (middle). A discontinuous function is never differentiable (right).
content.alt: Three coordinate planes arranged horizontally, illustrating the relationship between differentiability and continuity. The left panel shows y equals x squared, a smooth parabola that is both continuous and differentiable everywhere, with a tangent line drawn at x equals one. A checkmark indicates continuous, another checkmark indicates differentiable. The middle panel shows y equals the absolute value of x, forming a sharp V-shape at the origin. This function is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at x equals zero due to the corner. A checkmark indicates continuous, an X mark indicates not differentiable at zero. The right panel shows a step function that jumps from y equals zero to y equals one at x equals zero. This function is neither continuous nor differentiable at zero. An X mark indicates not continuous, another X mark indicates not differentiable. An arrow points from left to middle with the label differentiable implies continuous. Another arrow points from middle to right with an X through it, indicating that continuous does not imply differentiable.

A coordinate plane showing two functions. The teal curve is the parabola f of x equals x squared, opening upward with vertex at the origin. The warm-colored line is f prime of x equals 2x, passing through the origin with slope 2. Where the parabola decreases (left of origin), the derivative line is negative. Where the parabola increases (right of origin), the derivative line is positive.

A coordinate plane showing two functions. The teal curve is f of x equals x cubed, an S-shaped curve passing through the origin, decreasing to the left and increasing to the right. The warm-colored curve is f prime of x equals 3 x squared, a parabola opening upward with vertex at the origin. The derivative is always nonnegative, touching zero only at x equals 0 where the cubic has a horizontal tangent.

A smooth parabola-like curve labeled y equals f of x. The curve rises from the left, reaches a maximum near x equals 2, then falls toward the right. This is an exercise figure for sketching the derivative of a function that rises, levels off, and then falls.

marginNotes.text:
For since the creation of the world God's 
invisible qualities—his eternal power and divine nature—have been 
clearly seen, being understood from what has been made.

For God is not a God of disorder 
but of peace.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
Nothing takes place in the world whose 
meaning is not that of some maximum or minimum.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Barrow
marginNotes.text:
The only way to learn mathematics is to 
do mathematics.

marginNotes.author: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
marginNotes.text:
The art of discovering the causes 
of phenomena, or true hypotheses, is like the art of deciphering, in which 
an ingenious conjecture greatly shortens the road.
